evals.name: interpret-load-test-results
evals.prompt:
We ran a 60-minute load test with 200 concurrent users against our e-commerce API. Here are the results:
- p50: 180ms, p95: 1,240ms, p99: 3,800ms
- Error rate: 0.8%
- Throughput: 95 RPS
- SLA: p95 < 800ms, error rate < 1%

CPU stayed below 40%, memory was stable. The error rate was 0.8% throughout — no spikes.

What do these results mean?
evals.expected_output: Analysis identifies: (1) p95 SLA breach (1,240ms vs 800ms target) as a high/critical finding, (2) error rate is below SLA but constant-not-zero which is suspect, (3) the wide spread between p50=180ms and p95=1,240ms indicates a long-tail problem not a general slowness, (4) low CPU/stable memory rules out those root causes, (5) hypothesizes DB, external dependency, or lock contention as likely cause of tail latency. Severity classification included.

assertions.name: identifies_sla_breach
assertions.description: Correctly identifies that p95=1,240ms exceeds the 800ms SLA and classifies it as a failure

assertions.name: analyzes_latency_spread
assertions.description: Notes the wide spread between p50 (180ms) and p95 (1,240ms) and explains this indicates outliers/long-tail, not general slowness

assertions.name: uses_infrastructure_context
assertions.description: Uses the low CPU and stable memory data to rule out CPU saturation or memory leak as root causes

assertions.name: assigns_severity
assertions.description: Assigns a severity level (critical/high/medium/low) to the p95 SLA breach finding

assertions.name: proposes_root_cause_hypothesis
assertions.description: Proposes at least one specific root cause hypothesis (DB, external dependency, lock contention, or similar) not ruled out by the available data

evals.name: write-technical-report
evals.prompt:
Based on these load test results, write a technical report:
- Test: Load test, 200 users, 60 minutes, Gatling
- p50: 180ms, p95: 1,240ms, p99: 3,800ms (SLA: p95 < 800ms)
- Error rate: 0.8% (SLA: < 1%)
- Throughput: 95 RPS
- DB slow query log showed 3 queries consistently taking 900ms+ at peak
- CPU: 38%, Memory: stable
- Baseline (last sprint): p95 = 720ms, error rate 0.3%
evals.expected_output: Structured technical report with: SLA compliance table showing p95 FAIL, baseline regression table showing p95 regression, findings section with at least one HIGH/CRITICAL finding about DB slow queries with specific numbers, infrastructure observations noting healthy CPU/memory, and recommendations table with prioritized actions. Report uses specific numbers from the prompt.

assertions.name: has_sla_compliance_table
assertions.description: Report includes a table or section comparing actual vs target for p95 and error rate, with PASS/FAIL status

assertions.name: has_regression_comparison
assertions.description: Report compares current p95 (1,240ms) to baseline p95 (720ms) and calls out the regression (+72%)

assertions.name: finding_references_db_queries
assertions.description: At least one finding references the DB slow queries (900ms+) as the likely root cause for the p95 SLA breach

assertions.name: finding_has_severity
assertions.description: Each finding has an explicit severity label (CRITICAL, HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW, or INFO)

assertions.name: has_recommendations
assertions.description: Report includes a prioritized recommendations section with at least one concrete action (index optimization, query tuning, or similar)

evals.name: write-business-report
evals.prompt:
Write a business report for our VP of Product based on these load test results before next week's launch:
- 200 users tested, expected 180 at peak
- p95: 1,240ms (SLA: 800ms) — FAIL
- Error rate: 0.8% — PASS
- Root cause found: 3 DB queries missing indexes — fix takes 1 day
- CPU, memory all healthy
evals.expected_output: Business report that: (1) uses plain language with no raw percentile numbers or tool names, (2) translates p95 SLA breach into user impact language (e.g. '1 in 20 users waits longer than expected'), (3) gives a clear go/no-go recommendation, (4) states what needs to happen before launch in non-technical terms, (5) mentions the fix is available and low-effort.

assertions.name: no_raw_technical_numbers
assertions.description: Report does not use raw percentile notation (p95, p99) or tool-specific terms in stakeholder-facing sections — translates metrics to user experience language

assertions.name: translates_sla_breach_to_user_impact
assertions.description: Describes the p95 SLA breach in terms of user experience (e.g., proportion of users affected, what they experience) not as a raw millisecond comparison

assertions.name: gives_clear_verdict
assertions.description: Report explicitly states a launch recommendation: ready / not ready / ready with conditions

assertions.name: mentions_fix_availability
assertions.description: Report notes that a fix has been identified and can be applied before launch (database index fix)

assertions.name: no_jargon_in_business_sections
assertions.description: Business-facing sections do not contain technical jargon (no tool names, database terms, or percentile notation)

evals.name: error-rate-interpretation
evals.prompt:
Our load test showed:
- At 50 users: error rate 0%
- At 100 users: error rate 0%
- At 150 users: error rate 0.2%
- At 200 users: error rate 2.8%
- At 250 users: error rate 8.4%

Is this a bug or a capacity issue? What does the pattern mean?
evals.expected_output: Correctly identifies this as a capacity signal, not a bug — the error rate is zero at low load and grows with concurrency. Distinguishes this from a bug (which would show errors at all load levels). Identifies the breaking point between 150 and 200 users. Recommends investigating connection pool exhaustion, thread pool saturation, or DB connection limits as likely causes given the exponential growth pattern.

assertions.name: classifies_as_capacity_not_bug
assertions.description: Correctly identifies the load-correlated error growth as a capacity/saturation signal, not a code bug (which would appear at all load levels)

assertions.name: identifies_breaking_point
assertions.description: Identifies that the system starts degrading between 100 and 150 users (or 150-200 users), where errors first appear

assertions.name: explains_exponential_growth_significance
assertions.description: Notes that the exponential growth of error rate (0.2% → 2.8% → 8.4%) indicates approaching a hard limit — not linear degradation

assertions.name: proposes_specific_hypotheses
assertions.description: Suggests specific capacity-related root causes to investigate (connection pool, thread pool, DB connections, or similar — not generic 'server overload')

evals.name: stress-test-analysis
evals.prompt:
We ran a stress test to find our breaking point:
- 100 users: p95=320ms, 0% errors
- 200 users: p95=480ms, 0% errors
- 300 users: p95=890ms, 0.1% errors
- 400 users: p95=2,400ms, 4.2% errors
- 500 users: p95=8,100ms, 18% errors — system started returning 503s
- After reducing to 100 users: p95=340ms, 0% errors — full recovery in 90 seconds

What conclusions can we draw?
evals.expected_output: Identifies the breaking point between 300 and 400 users. Notes graceful degradation pattern (latency rises before errors appear) which is healthy behavior. Confirms system recovered — critical finding for stress tests. Gives the capacity number (~300 users or RPS equivalent) and notes the SLA compliance zone. Recommends operating below the identified breaking point with a safety margin.

assertions.name: identifies_breaking_point
assertions.description: Identifies the breaking point between 300 and 400 users where errors spike and latency becomes unacceptable

assertions.name: notes_graceful_degradation
assertions.description: Observes the progressive latency increase before error spike — identifies this as a positive sign of graceful degradation rather than sudden collapse

assertions.name: confirms_recovery
assertions.description: Explicitly notes and rates the system's recovery (p95 returned to 340ms, 0% errors) as a positive and important stress test finding

assertions.name: gives_safe_operating_capacity
assertions.description: Provides a safe operating capacity recommendation (at or below 300 users, or with a safety margin below the breaking point)

assertions.name: distinguishes_sla_zone
assertions.description: Identifies the load range where SLA is met (≤ 300 users based on results) vs. where it is breached

evals.name: endurance-memory-leak
evals.prompt:
We ran a 4-hour endurance test at 150 users. Here are the observations at different time intervals:

- 0:00 — p95: 420ms, memory: 2.1GB, error rate: 0%
- 1:00 — p95: 430ms, memory: 2.8GB, error rate: 0%
- 2:00 — p95: 510ms, memory: 3.6GB, error rate: 0%
- 3:00 — p95: 780ms, memory: 4.4GB, error rate: 0.1%
- 4:00 — p95: 1,650ms, memory: 5.3GB, error rate: 1.8%

SLA: p95 < 500ms, error rate < 0.5%. The service was restarted after the test.

What does this pattern tell us?
evals.expected_output: Correctly identifies memory leak pattern: monotonic memory growth over 4 hours, correlated with rising latency and eventually errors. Explains GC pressure increasing as heap fills. States this is an endurance-specific issue — would not be caught in a short load test. Classifies as CRITICAL (SLA breached, service would need restart in production). Recommends heap dump analysis, identifying unbounded caches or retained objects.

assertions.name: identifies_memory_leak_pattern
assertions.description: Correctly identifies the monotonic memory growth (2.1GB → 5.3GB) as a memory leak signature

assertions.name: correlates_memory_to_latency
assertions.description: Connects the memory growth to the progressive latency degradation — explains GC pressure or heap exhaustion as the mechanism

assertions.name: notes_endurance_specific
assertions.description: States that this issue would not appear in a short load test — it only manifests over time, which is why endurance testing is necessary

assertions.name: classifies_as_critical
assertions.description: Classifies the finding as CRITICAL or HIGH severity given the SLA is breached and the service would require periodic restarts in production

assertions.name: recommends_heap_analysis
assertions.description: Recommends heap dump analysis, profiling retained objects, or investigating unbounded caches as the investigation next step

evals.name: regression-detection
evals.prompt:
We ran the same load test (200 users, 30 minutes) before and after a release. Here are the comparison results:

Baseline (v2.3.0):
- p50: 140ms, p95: 680ms, p99: 1,100ms
- Error rate: 0.05%
- Throughput: 110 RPS

Current (v2.4.0):
- p50: 145ms, p95: 980ms, p99: 2,400ms
- Error rate: 0.05%
- Throughput: 108 RPS

SLA: p95 < 800ms. Is this a regression? Should we block the release?
evals.expected_output: Identifies: p95 regression of +44% (680ms → 980ms), p99 regression of +118% (1,100ms → 2,400ms), p50 essentially unchanged (+3.5%). Error rate and throughput unchanged. Concludes YES, regression detected — p95 SLA is now breached. The p50 vs p95 delta suggests a new tail-latency problem introduced in v2.4.0. Recommends blocking release pending investigation.

assertions.name: computes_regression_percentages
assertions.description: Calculates the p95 delta (+44%) and p99 delta (+118%) explicitly, not just notes they increased

assertions.name: flags_sla_breach
assertions.description: Identifies that p95=980ms breaches the 800ms SLA and was not breaching in baseline (680ms)

assertions.name: notes_p50_stable
assertions.description: Observes that p50 is essentially unchanged — indicating a tail-latency problem introduced in v2.4.0, not a general slowdown

assertions.name: recommends_block_release
assertions.description: Recommends blocking or not deploying v2.4.0 given the SLA breach regression

assertions.name: suggests_investigation_focus
assertions.description: Points to what changed in v2.4.0 that could cause tail latency: new code paths, new dependencies, configuration changes

evals.name: mean-vs-percentile-education
evals.prompt: My manager says our load test passed because the average response time was 280ms, well within our 500ms SLA. But I'm looking at the Locust report and p99 is 4,200ms. How do I explain to my manager that the test actually failed?
evals.expected_output: Explains the fundamental difference between mean and percentile with a concrete example of how a small number of slow requests can be hidden by a good mean. Translates p99=4,200ms to business impact (1 in 100 users waits 4.2 seconds). Provides language the user can use with their manager. Explains why percentile-based SLAs are standard in the industry for user experience measurement.

assertions.name: explains_mean_hides_outliers
assertions.description: Explains that the mean can be pulled down by many fast requests while slow requests remain hidden — providing a concrete example of this phenomenon

assertions.name: translates_p99_to_user_impact
assertions.description: Translates p99=4,200ms into user-facing language: 1 in 100 users waits 4.2 seconds — over 8× the SLA

assertions.name: gives_manager_communication_language
assertions.description: Provides specific language or framing the user can use to explain the issue to their manager without technical jargon

assertions.name: recommends_percentile_slas
assertions.description: Explains why percentile-based SLAs (p95, p99) are used instead of averages for measuring user experience in production systems

evals.name: k6-output-interpretation
evals.prompt:
Here is the k6 output from our load test. Can you analyze it?

```
scenarios: (100.00%) 1 scenario, 100 max VUs, 5m30s maximum duration
default: 100 looping VUs for 5m0s (gracefulStop: 30s)

✓ status was 200
✗ response body contains user
  ↳  61% — ✓ 1830 / ✗ 1170

checks.........................: 80.50%  ✓ 3660  ✗ 1170
http_req_blocked...............: avg=1.1ms  p(95)=8µs
http_req_duration..............: avg=340ms  p(90)=780ms  p(95)=1240ms  p(99)=3100ms
http_req_failed................: 0.00%   ✓ 0  ✗ 3000
http_req_waiting...............: avg=338ms  p(95)=1238ms
http_reqs......................: 3000    9.99/s
vus............................: 100     min=100    max=100

THRESHOLDS
http_req_duration.............: p(95)<1000 — FAIL
http_req_failed...............: rate<0.01  — PASS
```

Our SLA is p95 < 1000ms and error rate < 1%. Did we pass?
evals.expected_output: Explains that: (1) http_req_failed=0% means no HTTP errors — the threshold passes, BUT (2) checks=80.5% means the 'response body contains user' assertion failed 39% of the time — this is a business logic failure invisible to http_req_failed, (3) p95=1,240ms breaches the 1000ms threshold — confirmed by THRESHOLDS FAIL, (4) overall the test FAILED on two dimensions: SLA breach and failing checks. Explains the difference between http_req_failed and checks.

assertions.name: identifies_threshold_fail
assertions.description: Identifies that p(95)=1,240ms exceeds the 1000ms threshold and the THRESHOLDS section shows FAIL

assertions.name: distinguishes_checks_from_http_errors
assertions.description: Explains that http_req_failed=0% and checks=80.5% are different metrics — http_req_failed=0% means no HTTP errors, but failing checks means business logic assertions failed

assertions.name: identifies_body_assertion_failure
assertions.description: Notes that the 'response body contains user' check failed 39% of the time (1170/3000) — this is likely a functional bug, not a load issue

assertions.name: correct_overall_verdict
assertions.description: Concludes the test FAILED on at least two dimensions: p95 SLA breach and failing business-logic checks

evals.name: gatling-report-interpretation
evals.prompt:
I ran a Gatling load test and here are the stats from the HTML report's request breakdown table:

| Request | Total | OK | KO | % KO | p50 | p95 | p99 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| All Requests | 45,000 | 43,200 | 1,800 | 4.0% | 210ms | 1,850ms | 5,400ms |
| GET /api/products | 22,500 | 22,490 | 10 | 0.04% | 180ms | 480ms | 920ms |
| POST /api/orders | 9,000 | 7,650 | 1,350 | 15.0% | 280ms | 3,200ms | 7,100ms |
| GET /api/cart | 13,500 | 13,060 | 440 | 3.3% | 190ms | 1,100ms | 3,800ms |

SLA: p95 < 1000ms, error rate < 1%. What should I investigate?
evals.expected_output: Identifies that: (1) POST /api/orders is the critical problem — 15% error rate and p95=3,200ms — far exceeding SLA on both dimensions, (2) GET /api/cart also breaches p95 SLA (1,100ms vs 1,000ms target) and has 3.3% errors, (3) GET /api/products is healthy, (4) the global 4% error rate is almost entirely driven by /api/orders. Recommends investigating the order creation endpoint's write path — DB locks, validation, or downstream service calls.

assertions.name: identifies_orders_as_primary_problem
assertions.description: Identifies POST /api/orders as the primary failing endpoint — 15% error rate and p95=3,200ms vs 1,000ms SLA

assertions.name: identifies_cart_as_secondary
assertions.description: Identifies GET /api/cart as a secondary issue — p95 exceeds SLA (1,100ms > 1,000ms) and has 3.3% errors

assertions.name: notes_products_is_healthy
assertions.description: Notes that GET /api/products is within SLA and healthy — the problem is not global

assertions.name: explains_global_error_rate_composition
assertions.description: Explains that the global 4% error rate is driven primarily by the /api/orders endpoint, not a systemic problem

assertions.name: recommends_write_path_investigation
assertions.description: Recommends investigating the order creation write path — DB locks, downstream service calls, or validation logic — not a generic 'check the server'

evals.name: spike-test-recovery-analysis
evals.prompt:
We ran a spike test to prepare for our Black Friday launch. Here is the timeline:

- 0:00–2:00 — Baseline: 50 users, p95=320ms, 0% errors
- 2:00–2:30 — Spike to 500 users (30 seconds)
- 2:30–7:30 — Peak hold: 500 users, p95=2,800ms, error rate 6.2%, many 503s
- 7:30–8:00 — Load drops back to 50 users
- 8:00–9:00 — p95=890ms, 1.1% errors
- 9:00–11:00 — p95=430ms, 0.2% errors
- 11:00–13:00 — p95=335ms, 0% errors — back to baseline

Our SLA: p95 < 500ms, error rate < 1%. Is the system ready for Black Friday?
evals.expected_output: Identifies: (1) system breached SLA severely at spike peak (p95=2,800ms, 6.2% errors — both fail), (2) recovery was slow — took 4.5 minutes to return to baseline after load dropped, (3) the 30-second ramp to 500 users was too fast — the system could not absorb it, (4) system IS NOT ready for Black Friday as-is. Recommends: finding the bottleneck at peak load, scaling capacity, and verifying with a second spike test after fixes. Also notes positive: system eventually recovered — not a hard crash.

assertions.name: identifies_peak_sla_breach
assertions.description: Identifies that at peak (500 users), p95=2,800ms and error rate=6.2% both fail SLA targets

assertions.name: measures_recovery_time
assertions.description: Identifies that recovery took approximately 4.5 minutes (from 7:30 when load dropped to ~11:00 when baseline was restored) — this is slow

assertions.name: notes_positive_recovery
assertions.description: Notes that the system eventually recovered fully — this is a positive signal (graceful degradation and recovery, not a crash)

assertions.name: gives_not_ready_verdict
assertions.description: Concludes the system is NOT ready for Black Friday at the tested scale without addressing the capacity issues found at peak

assertions.name: recommends_bottleneck_investigation
assertions.description: Recommends investigating the bottleneck at 500 users (connection pool, thread pool, or DB) and retesting after fixes

evals.name: per-endpoint-analysis
evals.prompt:
Here are the per-endpoint results from our Locust test (200 users, 30 minutes). SLA: all endpoints p95 < 800ms, global error rate < 1%:

| Endpoint | Requests | Failures | Failure% | p50 | p95 | p99 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GET /api/products | 18,400 | 12 | 0.07% | 145ms | 380ms | 710ms |
| GET /api/product/:id | 9,200 | 8 | 0.09% | 160ms | 420ms | 850ms |
| GET /api/cart | 9,200 | 6 | 0.07% | 170ms | 390ms | 720ms |
| POST /api/cart/add | 6,100 | 4 | 0.07% | 190ms | 460ms | 810ms |
| GET /api/orders | 4,600 | 3 | 0.07% | 180ms | 410ms | 780ms |
| POST /api/checkout | 2,300 | 1,610 | 70.0% | 890ms | 8,400ms | 12,000ms |

Global: 49,800 requests, 1,643 failures, 3.3% error rate
evals.expected_output: Identifies that POST /api/checkout is catastrophically failing (70% error rate, p95=8,400ms) and is the sole cause of the global 3.3% error rate. All other endpoints are healthy and within SLA. The problem is isolated to the checkout write path. Recommends investigating the checkout endpoint specifically — not a global infrastructure problem. Global error rate of 3.3% is entirely from checkout.

assertions.name: identifies_checkout_as_sole_problem
assertions.description: Identifies POST /api/checkout as the only failing endpoint — 70% failure rate and p95=8,400ms — and all other endpoints are healthy

assertions.name: explains_global_rate_is_misleading
assertions.description: Explains that the global 3.3% error rate is almost entirely caused by /api/checkout — the other endpoints contribute < 0.1% errors collectively

assertions.name: confirms_other_endpoints_healthy
assertions.description: Confirms that the other 5 endpoints all pass SLA (p95 < 800ms, errors < 1%) — the problem is not systemic

assertions.name: recommends_checkout_specific_investigation
assertions.description: Recommends investigating the checkout endpoint specifically (write path, payment integration, inventory reservation, or DB transaction) — not a general infrastructure investigation

evals.name: incomplete-data-handling
evals.prompt:
We ran a load test with JMeter but the person who configured it only exported the Summary Report, not the Aggregate Report. All I have is:

- Total requests: 24,000
- Average response time: 410ms
- Min: 89ms
- Max: 18,400ms
- Error rate: 2.3%
- Throughput: 80 RPS

Our SLA is p95 < 1000ms and error rate < 1%. Did we pass?
evals.expected_output: Explains that with only mean/min/max data, it is IMPOSSIBLE to determine p95 SLA compliance. The mean of 410ms could coexist with a p95 of 2,000ms+ given the max of 18,400ms. The error rate of 2.3% clearly fails the 1% SLA. Recommends re-running the test with proper percentile output (Aggregate Report, Dashboard Report, or --out json). States what CAN be concluded: error rate fails SLA, and the max of 18,400ms indicates severe outliers, but percentile SLA compliance is unknown.

assertions.name: identifies_data_is_insufficient_for_p95
assertions.description: Clearly states that mean, min, and max are not sufficient to evaluate p95 SLA compliance — percentile data is required

assertions.name: confirms_error_rate_fail
assertions.description: Confirms that the 2.3% error rate clearly fails the 1% SLA — this can be determined from available data

assertions.name: explains_mean_cannot_proxy_p95
assertions.description: Explains why the 410ms mean does not tell us whether p95 is within 1000ms — given the 18,400ms max, the tail could be very long

assertions.name: recommends_rerun_with_percentiles
assertions.description: Recommends re-running the test with JMeter Aggregate Report, Dashboard Report generation, or JTL export to get proper percentile data

assertions.name: states_what_can_be_concluded
assertions.description: Clearly separates what IS known (error rate fails, max shows severe outliers) from what is UNKNOWN (p95 SLA compliance) — does not make up conclusions
